title: Garbage In, Garbage Out
concept: A model's quality is capped by the quality of the features it's given — no algorithm, however advanced, can invent good signal out of bad inputs.

options.text: Turning raw data into the input variables (features) a model can learn from
Choosing which color scheme an app uses
Buying a bigger server to store data

exercises.explain: Right! Now: what happens if that preparation step goes badly — if the features themselves are low quality?

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model can typically learn just as well from raw, unprepared data as from well-engineered features.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! And today we push that idea further: is there anything a clever algorithm can do to rescue bad features?

cards.title: A championship driver, a broken car
cards.body: Put the world's best race car driver behind the wheel of a car with flat tires and a near-empty tank. No matter how skilled the driver is, that car isn't winning the race today.

cards.title: What is "garbage in, garbage out"?
cards.body: Garbage in, garbage out (GIGO) means a model's output quality is capped by the quality of its input features. No matter how advanced the algorithm, it can only work with the signal it's actually given.

cards.title: The driver is the algorithm, the car is your features
cards.body: A brilliant algorithm is like that championship driver — skilled, but limited by the car it's given. Weak, noisy, or meaningless features are the flat tires and empty tank: no amount of driving skill fixes a broken vehicle.

cards.title: A readmission predictor with junk inputs
cards.body: A hospital feeds a state-of-the-art model features like patient ID number and a form-entry timestamp — neither actually relates to health outcomes. Even the most sophisticated algorithm can't find a real medical pattern in inputs that don't carry medical signal.

cards.title: A fancier algorithm won't save bad features
cards.body: It's tempting to think swapping in a more powerful algorithm can compensate for weak inputs. In reality, a better algorithm can squeeze a little more out of good features — it cannot invent signal that was never in the data to begin with.

cards.title: Low-quality vs. high-quality features
cards.body: Feature quality sets the ceiling on model quality:

left.text:
Meaningless IDs, noise
Caps performance low
No algorithm can rescue it

right.text:
Real, relevant signal
Raises the performance ceiling
Gives the algorithm something to work with

exercises.prompt: What does "garbage in, garbage out" mean for feature quality?

options.text: A model's output quality is capped by the quality of the features it's given
Models automatically discard any low-quality feature before training
More features always beats fewer features, regardless of quality
Garbage data only affects how long training takes, not accuracy

exercises.explain: Exactly! The quality of what goes in sets a ceiling on the quality of what comes out.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Switching to a more advanced algorithm can fully compensate for meaningless, low-signal features.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! A better algorithm can squeeze more out of good features, but it can't invent signal that isn't in the data at all.

exercises.sentence: No matter how advanced the algorithm, a model's quality is ___ by the quality of its input features.
exercises.explain: Right! Feature quality sets a hard ceiling that the algorithm alone can't raise.

exercises.prompt: Which situation is the clearest example of "garbage in, garbage out"?

options.text: A top-tier algorithm trained on features that are just random ID numbers still predicts poorly
A simple algorithm trained on carefully chosen, relevant features predicts reasonably well
A model takes slightly longer to train because the dataset is larger

exercises.explain: Correct! Even a top-tier algorithm can't overcome meaningless inputs — that's GIGO in action.

exercises.prompt: A team keeps upgrading to more powerful algorithms, but their model's accuracy barely improves. Based on GIGO, what should they investigate?

options.text: Whether their features actually carry meaningful, relevant signal in the first place
Whether their computer's screen resolution is high enough
Whether they should stop measuring accuracy altogether
Nothing — algorithm upgrades always guarantee better accuracy

exercises.explain: Right! If upgrading the algorithm isn't helping, the ceiling is likely being set by the features, not the algorithm.

exercises.prompt: A company's fraud-detection model uses only two features: a transaction's random ID number and the color theme of the app the customer used. Performance is poor. Their engineer proposes buying the most advanced algorithm on the market. What does GIGO predict will happen?

options.text: Performance will likely stay poor — neither feature carries real signal about fraud, so no algorithm can compensate
The advanced algorithm will fully fix the problem regardless of the features used
Performance will improve automatically just from having more computing power
The features will become more meaningful once a fancier algorithm is applied to them

exercises.explain: Exactly! Random IDs and app color themes carry no real fraud signal — GIGO says the algorithm can't manufacture signal that isn't there.

exercises.prompt: Two teams build fraud models. Team A uses a simple algorithm with well-chosen, relevant features. Team B uses a cutting-edge algorithm with meaningless features. Based on today's lesson, which team likely wins, and why?

options.text: Team A — relevant features give even a simple algorithm real signal to learn from, while Team B's advanced algorithm has nothing useful to work with
Team B — a cutting-edge algorithm always wins regardless of feature quality
It's a tie — algorithm choice and feature quality never actually interact
Neither — only the amount of raw data matters

exercises.explain: Right! Feature quality sets the ceiling — a simple algorithm with good features beats a fancy one starved of real signal. Next: let's see exactly how you create good features from raw data.
